sections.about: This metric measures water consumption per tonne of crude steel or comparable physical output and is disclosed under BRSR Principle 6 Essential Indicator water intensity in terms of physical output. It is the most operationally meaningful water metric for steel benchmarking because it normalises for production scale. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: JSW leads across all three years, improving from 2.45 kL/tcs in FY2023 to 2.39 kL/tcs in FY2024 and 2.369 kL/tcs in FY2025. SAIL improves from 3.06 kL/tcs in FY2023 to 3.02 kL/tcs in FY2024 but worsens sharply to 4.64 kL/tcs in FY2025. Tata improves from 5.1 kL/tcs in FY2023 to 4.40 kL/tcs in FY2024 before worsening to 4.76 kL/tcs in FY2025. Jindal is highest across all three years, improving from 7.39 kL/tcs in FY2023 to 7.26 kL/tcs in FY2024 before worsening to 7.36 kL/tcs in FY2025.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 3rd. JSW is best at 2.369 kL/tcs, making Tata's 4.76 kL/tcs approximately 101% higher. Tata ranks 3rd across all three years; intensity improves from 5.1 kL/tcs in FY2023 to 4.40 kL/tcs in FY2024 before worsening to 4.76 kL/tcs in FY2025, indicating that production growth and water-efficiency measures did not fully offset consumption growth.
sections.targets: Tata’s water strategy identifies minimising water usage and maximising recycling. SAIL’s ZLD initiatives are relevant but not expressed as a kL/tcs target.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata FY2022-23 intensity of 5.1 kL/tcs was not explicitly reported in the FY23 BRSR table and is sourced retrospectively from the FY24 BRSR report. SAIL FY2022-23 is reported in m³/tcs and stored as kL/tcs (1:1 conversion). This is the preferred metric for peer benchmarking versus absolute withdrawal or consumption volumes.
